notes: Started Chapter 1 covering foundational security concepts including the CIA triad, authenticity, and nonrepudiation. Focused on understanding how governance and policy frameworks guide security programs.
Continued Chapter 1 and reviewed how security governance aligns with business objectives. Took notes on policies, standards, procedures, and guidelines and how they differ in practice.
Studied threat modeling concepts and supply chain risk management. Interesting to see how governance structures shape how organizations identify and manage risk.
Finished Chapter 1 and summarized core governance ideas and security principles. Created a quick reference for CIA triad concepts and policy hierarchy.
Started Chapter 2 covering personnel security concepts and risk management foundations. Took notes on how human factors and insider threats influence security programs.
Reviewed risk management frameworks and the role of organizational policies in managing security risk. Noted how structured frameworks help standardize security decision making.
Focused on social engineering threats and how attackers exploit human behavior. Added examples of phishing and pretexting attacks to my notes.
Studied the importance of security awareness, training, and education programs. Interesting to see how culture and training influence overall security posture.
Finished Chapter 2 and summarized key personnel security practices and risk management ideas. Flagged social engineering defenses as an area worth revisiting.
Started Chapter 3 on business continuity planning. Reviewed the purpose of BCP and how organizations prepare for disruptions and operational outages.
Studied the business impact analysis process and how organizations identify critical systems and acceptable downtime. Noted the importance of recovery objectives.
Continued Chapter 3 with continuity strategy development and recovery planning. Interesting to see how technical and business priorities must align.
Finished Chapter 3 and summarized the full BCP lifecycle including planning, implementation, and approval. Wrote out a quick checklist of BCP steps.
Started Chapter 4 reviewing legal systems and how laws influence cybersecurity practices. Took notes on criminal, civil, and administrative law categories.
Continued Chapter 4 with privacy laws, regulatory requirements, and compliance obligations. Interesting how different jurisdictions approach data protection.
Reviewed contracts, procurement considerations, and legal responsibilities around information security. Noted how vendor relationships affect compliance risk.
Worked through examples of compliance frameworks and regulatory oversight. Added notes comparing regulatory requirements to internal security policies.
Finished Chapter 4 and summarized major legal and regulatory concepts relevant to cybersecurity. Created a quick reference list of common compliance drivers.
Started Chapter 5 covering asset security and information classification. Reviewed how organizations identify and categorize sensitive data.
Studied asset ownership roles and responsibilities along with handling requirements. Interesting how classification decisions influence control selection.
Focused on data protection mechanisms and secure handling practices throughout the information lifecycle.
Finished Chapter 5 and summarized asset classification models and security baselines used to protect sensitive information.
Started Chapter 6 reviewing the foundations of cryptography. Took notes on encryption concepts and the role cryptography plays in protecting data.
Studied symmetric encryption algorithms and how secret keys are used to protect confidentiality. Noted common algorithm examples and their tradeoffs.
Reviewed cryptographic lifecycle concepts including key generation, distribution, rotation, and destruction.
Finished Chapter 6 and summarized symmetric cryptography concepts and where encryption fits into overall system security.
Started Chapter 7 focusing on asymmetric cryptography and public key infrastructure concepts.
Studied digital signatures, hashing algorithms, and the role they play in integrity and authentication.
Reviewed PKI components including certificate authorities, registration authorities, and certificate management.
Finished Chapter 7 and summarized how hybrid cryptography combines symmetric and asymmetric methods in real-world systems.
Started Chapter 8 covering secure system design principles and foundational security models.
Reviewed common security models and how they enforce confidentiality, integrity, and access control.
Studied system security capabilities and the role of controls in enforcing secure design principles.
Finished Chapter 8 and summarized secure architecture concepts and design techniques for protecting systems.
Started Chapter 9 reviewing common security vulnerabilities and threat categories.
Studied vulnerabilities across modern architectures including cloud, IoT, and containerized systems.
Reviewed common attack methods and countermeasures used to protect client, server, and distributed systems.
Finished Chapter 9 and summarized key vulnerability patterns and defensive strategies.
Reviewed Chapter 1 and completed the end-of-chapter practice questions. Focused on reinforcing governance concepts, the CIA triad, and how policies and standards support security programs.
Reviewed Chapter 2 and worked through the practice questions. Helped clarify personnel security concepts and how training and awareness programs reduce social engineering risk.
Reviewed Chapter 3 and completed the practice questions. Revisited business impact analysis concepts and the key steps in business continuity planning.
Reviewed Chapter 4 and completed the chapter questions. Focused on distinguishing legal frameworks, privacy regulations, and compliance responsibilities.
Reviewed Chapter 5 and completed the practice questions. Reinforced asset classification concepts and how handling requirements protect sensitive information.
Reviewed Chapter 6 and completed the end-of-chapter questions. Revisited symmetric cryptography concepts and key management fundamentals.
Reviewed Chapter 7 and completed the practice questions. Focused on PKI components, digital signatures, and how asymmetric cryptography supports authentication and integrity.
Reviewed Chapter 8 and worked through the practice questions. Reinforced secure design principles and how security models influence system architecture decisions.
Reviewed Chapter 9 and completed the practice questions. Focused on common vulnerability patterns and the countermeasures used to protect modern systems.
Reviewed concepts and definitions from Chapters 1–9 based on questions I answered incorrectly during the end-of-chapter practice reviews.

Started Chapter 10 on physical security requirements. Reviewed secure facility planning, critical path analysis, and how infrastructure dependencies like power, environmental controls, and networking support mission-critical systems. Also studied Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design (CPTED) principles and how environmental design combined with traditional controls can help deter and detect physical threats.

Continued Chapter 10 on physical security controls. Reviewed facility and site security controls including access control systems, alarms, cameras, guards, and layered physical defenses designed to deter, detect, delay, and respond to intrusions. Also studied cable plant security, protected distribution systems (PDS), server room placement, smartcard authentication, and reliability concepts such as MTTF, MTTR, and MTBF related to equipment failure and availability.
Physical security chapter continued. Learned about securing wiring clostets, smartcards, badges, intrusion detection systems, and media storage facilities. This is a long chapter.
Physical security chapter completed. Learned about utility security considerations. The main points had to do with power, fire, noise, water, and lighting.

The OSI model was introduced. The 7 layers (physical, data link, network, transport, session, presentation, and application) were explained. A useful saying to remember the layers is: All Presidents Since Truman Never Did Pottery.

The TCP/IP model was introduced. The 4 layers (link, internet, transport, and application) were explained. Common application protocols were mentioned, such as HTTP, FTP, SMTP, and POP3. TCP uses a three-way handshake to establish a connection. UDP was also mentioned and is a connectionless protocol.

DNS was introduced. It is a distributed system for resolving domain names to IP addresses. It is a hierarchical system, with the root domain at the top level. A DNS server can be authoritative for a domain. DNS poisoning is a type of attack where an attacker modifies DNS records to redirect users to a malicious website.

Internet Protocol (IP) was introduced. It is a connectionless protocol that does not guarantee delivery or order of packets. It is a best-effort delivery protocol. IP addresses are 32-bit values used to identify a device on a network. IPv4 is the most common version of IP, while IPv6 is the newer version and uses 128-bit addresses. ARP was also introduced and is used to find the MAC address of a device on a network.

Network segmentation was discussed. It is the process of dividing a network into smaller networks to improve security and performance. Segmentation can be done at the physical, data link, network, or application layer, though it is most commonly done at the network layer using routers. There are physical, logical, and hybrid segmentation approaches. Microsegmentation is a more granular form of logical segmentation, typically enforced with firewalls to control traffic between segments. It is used to improve security and support regulatory compliance.

Started the wireless networking section and reviewed how wireless shares many of the same threats as wired networks, with added exposure to eavesdropping and denial-of-service because signals are unbounded. Studied 802.11 basics, ad hoc vs. infrastructure mode, and different access point deployment models including standalone, bridge, and extended modes. Also reviewed fat vs. thin access points and how centralized wireless controllers improve management and security. Finished by covering SSIDs, ESSIDs, BSSIDs, beacon frames, and why hiding the SSID is not real security.

Continued wireless networking with channel use, interference, site surveys, and how wireless cells are assessed for signal strength, quality, and coverage. Studied wireless security models including open system and shared key authentication, then reviewed WEP, WPA, WPA2, and WPA3 and how each improved on the last. Also covered enterprise authentication with 802.1X and EAP, along with LEAP, PEAP, and why WPS and MAC filtering are weak security measures. Finished with antenna types, captive portals, and general wireless hardening practices like firmware updates, changing defaults, and using WPA2 or WPA3.

Reviewed wireless communication methods including FHSS, DSSS, OFDM, MIMO, TDMA, and CDMA, and how they improve signal use and throughput. Also studied Bluetooth, RFID, and NFC, along with common Bluetooth attack types such as bluesnarfing, bluejacking, and bluesmacking. Covered wireless attack techniques including rogue access points, evil twin attacks, disassociation, adversary-in-the-middle, jamming, replay, and IV attacks, along with defenses like WIDS and using VPNs on wireless connections. Finished with notes on satellite and cellular communications and the role of CDNs in improving content delivery performance.

Moved into secure network design and reviewed intranets, extranets, screened subnets, and screened hosts, including how firewalls separate trusted and untrusted zones. Studied common network equipment such as repeaters, hubs, bridges, switches, routers, multiplexers, jump boxes, sensors, collectors, aggregators, and system-on-a-chip devices. Also covered network access control (NAC) and how it enforces policy before or after admission to the network. Finished with firewall concepts including packet filtering, application and circuit-level firewalls, stateful inspection, next-generation firewalls, and internal segmentation firewalls.

Finished Chapter 11 by reviewing network media, fiber optic cabling, transport architecture, and common physical network topologies including ring, bus, star, and mesh. I also studied Ethernet concepts, analog vs. digital signaling, synchronous vs. asynchronous transmission, baseband vs. broadband, and communication methods such as broadcast, multicast, unicast, anycast, and geocast. The chapter wrapped up with LAN media access methods including CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA, token passing, and polling, which helped clarify how shared networks manage access and avoid collisions. Overall, this chapter gave me a much stronger foundation in how networks are structured, how data moves through them, and how communication is controlled across different media and topologies.

Authentication protocols include PPP with PAP, CHAP, and EAP, while port security restricts device access through controls like IEEE 802.1X. QoS manages network performance using bandwidth, latency, jitter, packet loss, throughput, and signal-to-noise ratio. PSTN, VoIP, vishing, phreaking, and PBX abuse show why voice communications need encryption, awareness, and security controls.

Remote access methods and collaboration tools require policies covering authentication, encryption, monitoring, and secure usage. Load balancing uses techniques like round robin, VIPs, and active-active or active-passive designs for scalability and failover. Email systems lack native security and face spoofing and DoS risks, mitigated by S/MIME, PGP, SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and TLS.

VPNs use tunneling and protocols like IPsec to secure traffic, while VLANs segment networks but can be attacked through VLAN hopping. MAC attacks include flooding and cloning/spoofing, mitigated by MAC limits, filtering, and intrusion detection. NAT/PAT hides internal networks and enables many clients through translation, while APIPA, loopback, circuit switching, packet switching, virtual circuits, and MOU/ISA agreements are key network concepts.
Reviewed my notes from Chapter 10 and studied the end of chapter summary + important terms.
Reviewed my notes from Chapter 11 and studied the end of chapter summary + important terms. This chapter had many acronyms to memorize.
Reviewed my notes from Chapter 12 and studied the end of chapter summary + important terms.

Started chapter 13 on Managing Identity and Access Management (IAM). This chapter covers the fundamentals of identity and access management, including the different types of identities, the different types of access, and the different types of access management. It also covers the different types of identity and access management policies, and the different types of identity and access management systems.
Completed chapter 13 on Managing Identity and Access Management (IAM).

Started chapter 14 on Controlling and Monitoring Access, covering the distinctions between permissions, rights, and privileges along with authorization mechanisms like implicit deny, access control matrices, and capability lists. Reviewed core security principles including need-to-know, least privilege, and separation of duties, plus constrained interfaces and content/context-dependent controls. Covered the main access control models (DAC, RBAC, Rule-Based, ABAC, MAC, and Risk-Based) and the MAC model variations (hierarchical, compartmentalized, and hybrid).

Finished chapter 14 on Controlling and Monitoring Access, covering permissions/rights/privileges, authorization mechanisms (implicit deny, access control matrix, capability lists, constrained interfaces, content and context-dependent controls), and the main access control models (DAC, RBAC, Rule-Based, ABAC, MAC, Risk-Based). Reviewed SSO implementations including SAML, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, Kerberos, RADIUS, and TACACS+, plus the zero trust components (subjects, policy engines, policy administrators, and policy enforcement points). Studied common access control attacks like privilege escalation, password attacks (dictionary, brute force, spraying, credential stuffing, birthday, rainbow table), pass-the-hash, Mimikatz, sniffing, and spoofing, along with core protection methods like salting/peppering, MFA, account lockout, and password masking.
Started chapter 15 on Security Assessment and Testing.

Continue chapter 15 on Security Assessment and Testing, covering the three components of a security assessment program (security tests, assessments, and audits), including SOC 1/2/3 engagements, Type I/II reports, and audit frameworks like COBIT and ISO. Reviewed vulnerability assessment tools and the SCAP framework (CVE, CVSS, CCE, CPE, XCCDF, OVAL), plus the four categories of vulnerability scans: network discovery, network vulnerability, web vulnerability, and database vulnerability scanning. Covered the vulnerability management workflow (detection, validation, remediation), key TCP port numbers, and an introduction to penetration testing.

Finished chapter 15 on Security Assessment and Testing, covering software testing methods including code review (Fagan process), SAST, DAST, fuzz testing (mutation vs. generational), interface testing across APIs/UIs/network/physical, misuse case testing, and test coverage analysis (branch, condition, function, statement). Reviewed penetration testing phases from NIST SP 800-115 (planning, discovery, attack, reporting), white/gray/black box test types, and website monitoring via passive and synthetic methods. Wrapped up with red/blue/white team exercises, security management processes (log reviews, account management, DR/BC testing, training), and tracking key performance and risk indicators like open vulnerabilities and compromised accounts.

Started chapter 16 on Managing Security Operations, covering foundational concepts like due care vs. due diligence, need-to-know, least privilege, segregation of duties, two-person control, job rotation, mandatory vacations, PAM, and SLAs. Reviewed personnel safety and security topics including duress systems, travel safety, emergency management, and security awareness training. Began asset security covering information and asset ownership, hardware/software/intangible asset inventories, and tracking methods like barcodes and RFID tags.

Completed chapter 16, read up on personnel safety (duress systems, travel safety, emergency management), and asset management (hardware/software/intangible inventories tracked via barcodes and RFID). Covered cloud services including SaaS/PaaS/IaaS shared responsibility models, the four cloud deployment types (public, private, community, hybrid), serverless architecture, and the difference between scalability and elasticity. Studied configuration management (provisioning, baselining, automation), change management (6-step process rooted in ITIL), patch management (5-step process), vulnerability management, and the CVE dictionary as a standard for identifying and describing vulnerabilities.

Started chapter 17 on Preventing and Responding to Incidents, covering the goal of incident management (minimize organizational impact) and the 7-step lifecycle: Detection, Response, Mitigation, Reporting, Recovery, Remediation, and Lessons Learned. Reviewed each phase in detail including detection tools alerting IT staff, mitigation containing the incident, reporting obligations under law, recovery returning systems to full function, and remediation performing root cause analysis to prevent recurrence. Began covering preventive and detection controls, basic preventive measures (patching, removing unneeded services, IDS/IPS, antimalware, firewalls), and an introduction to botnets and denial of service attacks.

Completed chapter 17. This chapter focused on Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS). It covered the importance of logging and monitoring systems. It also covered ways to automage incident response.
